Charles, Grand Duke of Baden, was a prominent figure who spoke German fluently and was married to Stéphanie de Beauharnais. He was the son of Prince Karl Ludwig, Hereditary Prince of Baden, who was born in Karlsruhe and passed away in Arboga, Sweden. Charles’s mother, Karoline Luise of Hesse-Darmstadt, held the noble title of margrave, which is equivalent to a marquess. Karoline Luise’s artistic legacy is preserved in the Frick Art Research Library, managed by Stephen J. Bury, who was born in Blackburn, Lancashire. The library’s catalog, an online public access catalog, is operated by the Frick Art Research Library itself.

Charles, Grand Duke of Baden, was honored with the Order of St. Andrew, a prestigious award named after Andrew the Apostle, who lived during the Roman Empire and was a student of Jesus. The order originated in the Russian Empire, whose most populous city, Saint Petersburg, was founded by Peter the Great. Peter the Great’s political influence extended to the politics of Russia, which replaced the politics of the Soviet Union. His mother, Natalya Naryshkina, was born in Moscow and held the title of empress consort.

Stéphanie de Beauharnais, Charles’s spouse, passed away in Nice, France, and was buried at St. Michael Pforzheim in Germany. Her life is documented in the Allgemeine Deutsche Biographie, a public domain publication edited by the Historische Commission bei der königl. Akademie der Wissenschaften and published by Duncker & Humblot, a German company based in Berlin.

The city of Karlsruhe, where Charles’s father was born, is named after Karl III Wilhelm of Baden-Durlach, who died in Karlsruhe and was married to Magdalena Wilhelmine of Württemberg, a female politician. The title of margrave is associated with the march, while the marquess, depicted in the Camera dei Marchesi in Italy, directs the Marquisate. The Camera dei Marchesi is part of the Palazzo Ducale Mantua and focuses on the Marquess as its main subject.

The Communist Party of the Soviet Union followed the ideological lineage of the *Russian Social Democratic Labour Party (bolshevik)*, commonly known as the Bolsheviks. The Bolsheviks, named after the concept of "majority," were a left-wing political faction led by Vladimir Lenin, who had black hair, as noted in the *Great Soviet Encyclopedia (1926–1947)*. N. Tupolev*. His native language was Russian, reflecting the linguistic and political ties within the Soviet Union.

input: Gace Brulé, a human, was known for writing and speaking in French. French, a language written in the Latin script, holds an Ethnologue language status of "0 International," indicating its widespread and secure use. This status is part of the Expanded Graded Intergenerational Disruption Scale, a measurement scale studied in linguistics. On this scale, "0 International" is the highest rank, followed by "1 National," which is used for languages with national significance. Below "1 National" is "2 Provincial," representing languages with regional importance, and then "3 Wider Communication," which denotes languages used for broader communication. Each of these ranks is interconnected, with "1 National" being the next lower rank from "0 International" and the next higher rank for "2 Provincial." Similarly, "3 Wider Communication" is positioned below "2 Provincial" and above "4 Educational." The main Wikimedia portal for French is "Wiktionary:About French," which serves as a dedicated resource for the language.

Nebel is notable for its list of Cultural Monuments, a heritage site cataloged in a Wikimedia list article. Target 11.4 is a facet of Sustainable Development Goal 11 and is followed by Target 11.5.

Amrum, where Nebel is located, is adjacent to the Wadden Sea, a body of water recognized as a National Geotope in Germany. The "Mechlenburg" family name, like North Frisian, is also written using the Latin script. Germany's economy is the subject of its own economic analysis.

Hans von Seeckt, born in Schleswig, was a loyal figure of the German Empire. Schleswig, located near the Schlei, a body of water in Schleswig-Holstein, is part of the Angeln region, which lies within the Schleswig-Holstein Uplands. The Schlei is fed by the Füsinger Au, a stream named after the village of Füsing in the municipality of Schaalby. After his death, von Seeckt was buried in the Invalids' Cemetery, a cultural heritage monument in Germany founded by Frederick II of Prussia. Frederick II, an advocate of enlightened absolutism—a political ideology rooted in the Age of Enlightenment—was buried at Sanssouci Palace, described in *The Nuttall Encyclopædia*, a public domain literary work.

The Invalids' Cemetery, located in Germany, was established during the reign of Frederick II, whose works are now in the public domain. Germany, governed by a constitutional monarchy during the German Empire, was led by the King of Prussia, who resided in Berlin Palace, owned by the House of Hohenzollern. The German Empire’s flag, with an aspect ratio of 5:3, was later replaced by the black-red-gold flag of Germany, as described in *Meyers Konversations-Lexikon*. This encyclopedia, published in the German Reich, is a significant historical source.

The German Reich maintained diplomatic relations with the Ukrainian People's Republic, which was governed by the Council of People's Ministers and later replaced by modern Ukraine. The Republic’s constitution, legislated by the Central Rada, was a foundational legal text. The Council of People's Ministers included positions like the Minister of Highways, held by Serhii Tymoshenko.

Schleswig-Holstein, with its capital in Kiel, is governed by the Minister-President, whose list of officeholders is documented in a Wikimedia list article. The region is part of the Northeast German Lake District and is home to the Schlei and Angeln. The Spreeinsel, where Berlin Palace is located, is in the Bezirk Mitte district of Berlin, near Bellevue Palace, the official residence of the president of Germany.

The Kingdom of Prussia, which followed the Duchy of Prussia, was a Protestant state that later became the Free State of Prussia, a member of the Weimar Republic. The Duchy of Prussia, centered in Königsberg (now Kaliningrad), succeeded the State of the Teutonic Order, governed by the Grand Master of the Teutonic Knights. This rich historical tapestry connects figures like Hans von Seeckt to the broader narratives of German and European history.

Europe, the continent where Poland and the Second Polish Republic are located, has its own regional economy and demographics. Warsaw, the capital of the Second Polish Republic, is situated in the Masovian Voivodeship, which has its own distinct economy.

The Kingdom of Poland replaced the earlier Kingdom of Poland (1320–1386), which had Kraków as its capital. The dictionary’s publisher, Brockhaus–Efron, is described in the Great Soviet Encyclopedia (1926–1947), a Russian-language encyclopedia.

The Kingdom of Poland’s capital was Gniezno, a city in Poland, and its legislative body, the Sejmik, was a legislative house described in the Brockhaus and Efron Encyclopedic Dictionary.

Eleanor of Castile, a human blessed by the Catholic Church, was the mother of Eleanor of England and passed away at the Abbey of Santa María la Real de Las Huelgas in Burgos, Spain. Her canonization status as "blessed" is connected to the process of beatification, which requires a positio, a detailed document scrutinized by the Church. This status partially coincides with the Islamic concept of "blessed in Islam," which is linked to the term "Tabarak'Allah," maintained by WikiProject Islam and attributed to God in Islam. "Tabarak'Allah" is synonymous with "tasbih," a form of Islamic prayer.

God in Islam, a central figure in the religion, is associated with the concept of Uluhiyah, which emphasizes monotheism and includes Tawhid al-Uluhiya as a core component. Islam, originating in the Hejaz region, is rooted in the Qur’an and uses Arabic as its primary language. The Abbey of Santa María la Real de Las Huelgas, where Eleanor of Castile died, is an example of Cistercian architecture and is located in Burgos. Burgos is known for its patron saint, Adelelmus of Burgos, a Benedictine abbot and religious figure canonized as a saint. The Benedictines, a religious order founded by Benedict of Nursia, include the Nuns of the Order of Saint Benedict, who reside in Benedictine nunneries and are documented in Meyers Konversations-Lexikon.

Eleanor of England, the daughter of Eleanor of Castile, was married to Alfonso VIII of Castile and was buried in the same abbey as her mother. Alfonso VIII, who spoke Spanish, was the son of Blanche of Navarre, Queen of Castile, and Sancho III of Castile. Blanche of Navarre was the daughter of Marguerite de l'Aigle, who was buried in Pamplona Cathedral and was a citizen of the Kingdom of France. Marguerite's mother, Juliette du Perche, was married to Gilbert de l'Aigle and was the daughter of Geoffrey II, Count of Perche.

The Kingdom of England, where Eleanor of England held citizenship, was succeeded by the Kingdom of Great Britain, which is represented by the flag of Great Britain and the anthem "God Save the King," composed by Henry Carey. The Kingdom of England also owned Fort Metal Cross in Ghana, located in the Ahanta West Municipal District. Ghana, situated in Africa, is governed by the Government of Ghana and is part of the continent studied under European studies, which focuses on the geography and culture of Europe.

The Abbey of Santa María la Real de Las Huelgas, a site of historical and religious significance, reflects the deep intertwining of European history, religion, and culture, from the Benedictine order to the legacy of Eleanor of Castile and her descendants.

The Palace of Versailles, founded by Louis XIII of France, features accessible toilets equipped with changing tables for diaper changes and disability handrails. The Order of Saint-Michel is part of a broader hierarchy of French orders, including the Order of the Holy Spirit, founded by Henry III of France, and the Order of Saint Louis, named after Louis IX of France. Louis IX, a member of the Capetian dynasty, was married to Margaret of Provence, who was buried in the Basilica of Saint-Denis.

The *Marche Henri IV* is also associated with the Kingdom of France, which used French as its primary language. French, maintained by WikiProject French, evolved from Middle French through pre-classical and Classical French. The architectural and cultural heritage of France is preserved in institutions like the Museum of the History of France, which showcases significant historical figures and events.

The Russian Empire, a historical country, had a flag that was based on the flag of the Netherlands and was created by Peter the Great. This flag, with a 3:2 aspect ratio, depicts a field and is characterized by landscape orientation, the opposite of portrait orientation, which is associated with a 2:3 aspect ratio.

Peter the Great, who died of natural causes, was the son of Alexei I of Russia. Alexei I, a native Russian speaker, was buried in the Cathedral of the Archangel in Moscow's Tverskoy District, a site dedicated to Eastern Orthodoxy and named after Archangel Michael. Alexei I was also the owner of the Capelines of the Tsars of Russia, a group of artifacts now housed in the Kremlin Armoury. Moscow, the capital of the Tverskoy District, has its own flag and is part of the Moscow province.

The Russian language, which Alexei I spoke, is considered safe by UNESCO and is taught by Russian teachers. Russia itself is a country with right-sided railway traffic and is home to Mount Elbrus, the highest peak in the country. The flag of the Netherlands, which served as the inspiration for the Russian flag, has a 3:2 aspect ratio and is the national flag of the Netherlands. The Netherlands, governed by the Cabinet of the Netherlands, has the motto "Je maintiendrai" (I will maintain) in French and is known for its economy. The Frunzensky District, named after Mikhail Frunze, a general born in Bishkek, Kyrgyzstan, is part of Saint Petersburg. Hermann von der Lieth-Thomsen was a military officer who held the rank of general. In the military hierarchy, the rank of general is immediately above lieutenant general, which itself is above major general. These ranks correspond to specific NATO codes for grades: general is classified as OF-9, lieutenant general as OF-8, and major general as OF-7. The NATO code system organizes military ranks in a structured hierarchy, with OF-9 being directly below OF-10 and above OF-8. Similarly, OF-8 is situated between OF-9 and OF-7, and OF-7 is positioned between OF-8 and OF-6. This hierarchical structure continues downward, with OF-6 being below OF-7 and above OF-5.

Curt Lincoln is a sports figure associated with Finland, where he received the Medal of Merit in silver with a golden cross from the Finnish Sports, an award conferred by the Ministry of Education and Culture. He is the father of Nina Rindt, who speaks Finnish and carries the family name Rindt. The name "Rindt" is written in the Latin script and is a recognized family name. Alexander's mother is Sheila van Meurs, and his father is Rowland Hood, the 3rd Viscount Bridport. Rowland served in the Royal Navy, part of His Majesty's Naval Service, and worked as a politician. His father, Maurice Hood, was married to Ethel Kendall and was the son of Arthur Hood, the 2nd Viscount Bridport.

Finland, where Curt Lincoln is active in sports, is located next to the Baltic Sea, which replaced the Littorina Sea. The Baltic Sea's drainage basin is part of the Atlantic Ocean drainage basin and shares a border with the North Sea basin. The Finnish language, spoken by Nina Rindt, is regulated by the Finnish Language Office, a department within the Institute for the Languages of Finland, headquartered in Hakaniemi, a neighborhood in Helsinki. The Institute is located in Siltasaari, part of Hakaniemi.

The United Kingdom, the country of citizenship for Alexander Hood, has the national anthem "God Save the King," written in G major with lyrics by Henry Carey. The UK's cultural heritage includes works in the collection of the Australian National Maritime Museum, located in Pyrmont and operated by the Department of Communications and the Arts. The museum is described in the Open GLAM Survey.

This cathedral, designed by the Italian architect Gaetano Chiaveri, is a prime example of baroque architecture and is adjacent to Dresden Castle, a heritage monument in Saxony, Germany. Germany, where German is the official language, operates the Embassy of Germany in Riga, Latvia. The embassy, which is part of the Govdirectory project funded by Wikimedia Deutschland and sponsored by Wikimedia Sverige, is headed by the ambassador of Germany to Latvia, whose official residence is located in Riga.

Riga, the capital of Latvia, is a European Capital of Culture and has Latvian as its official language. Latvia is known for being a free country. Meanwhile, Saxony, a parliamentary republic in Germany, is represented by its flag and governed by the Minister-President, currently Michael Kretschmer. Saxony is also a member of the IT Planning Council, which operates under the IT State Treaty.

Gaetano Chiaveri, born in Rome, was an Italian-speaking architect whose works are now in the public domain. Rome, known for its rich history and economy, is symbolized by the hashtag #Roma and is deeply connected to the ancient Roman civilization, which flourished in the Mediterranean Basin. Italian, a language with a nominative case, is studied by Italianists, professionals in the field of Italian studies.

Baroque architecture, influenced by the Counter-Reformation, stands in contrast to the Protestant Reformation, which included the English Reformation initiated by Henry VIII of England. The Roman Rite, used by the Latin Church headquartered in Vatican City, is opposite to the Byzantine Rite. The Latin Church, which practices Catholicism, includes the Roman Catholic tradition. In Poland, the Blessed Mother Teresa of Calcutta church in Kamionki honors Mother Teresa, a female member of the Missionaries of Charity.

Lutheranism, named after Martin Luther, whose archives are held at ETH Zurich University Archives, is a significant Christian tradition. ETH Zurich University Archives, located on Rämistrasse in Zurich, is part of the ETH Library and a member of Kalliope, an organization based in Berlin.

The House of Wettin, founded by Theodoric I of Wettin, originates from the Kingdom of Saxony and is named after Wettin Castle, an architectural heritage monument in Germany. The Kingdom of Saxony was part of the German Reich, which had diplomatic relations with the Ukrainian People’s Republic, led by Symon Petliura. Europe, symbolized by its flag, is home to Leipzig, a Fairtrade Town and the publication place of *Meyers Konversations-Lexikon*. This encyclopedia, published by the Bibliographisches Institut Leipzig, is a significant reference work in the German language.

Theodoric I of Wettin, an aristocrat also known as Dietrich, is documented in Wiktionary, a project by Jimmy Wales distributed via Google Play. Aristocracy, closely related to nobility, is a field practiced by aristocrats.

Alexander Graf is a human and a professional chess player who holds citizenship in Germany, where he also represents the country in sports. Chess players, like Graf, are part of the broader chess terminology, which includes a glossary of chess terms that are essential to the sport. Chess itself has a rich history, having been inspired by the ancient game of shatranj, which was practiced by Alaadin al Tabriz, a native of Tabriz. Shatranj, in turn, traces its origins to chaturanga, an early form of chess that originated in India. 

The world of chess is well-documented through various lists, including the list of chess players, which is a Wikimedia list of persons by occupation. This list also includes specialized subcategories, such as the list of Jewish chess players, which focuses on individuals of a specific ethnicity and profession.
